passage: Epidemiologists investigating antibiotic resistance tracked the percentage of bacterial infections resistant to first-line antibiotics across hospital settings of varying sizes. Their research suggests that the relationship between hospital size and resistance rates is not linear: while resistance rates increase from small to mid-size hospitals, the largest hospitals show a reversal of this trend, likely due to more rigorous stewardship programs.
stem: Which choice most effectively uses data from the figure to support the epidemiologists' claim about the non-linear relationship?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Resistance rates rose from 18% in small hospitals (under 100 beds) to 34% in mid-size hospitals (100-300 beds), but then dropped to 24% in large hospitals (over 300 beds), creating a pattern where the largest facilities had lower resistance than mid-size ones.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Small hospitals had an 18% resistance rate while large hospitals had a 24% rate, indicating that resistance generally increases with hospital size across the full range of facilities studied.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mid-size hospitals had the highest resistance rate at 34%, which was nearly double the 18% rate observed in small hospitals, suggesting that increasing hospital capacity dramatically raises resistance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Large hospitals had a resistance rate of 24%, which was 10 percentage points lower than the 34% rate in mid-size hospitals but still 6 points higher than the 18% rate in small hospitals.

explanation: The claim is about a non-linear pattern: resistance increases from small to mid-size, then decreases from mid-size to large. Choice A captures both directions -- the rise from 18% to 34% and the drop to 24% -- explicitly describing the non-linear pattern. Choice B omits the mid-size peak and implies a simple linear increase. Choice C focuses only on the small-to-midsize increase without addressing the reversal. Choice D presents the same numbers as A but frames them neutrally without explicitly identifying the non-linear pattern, and it could be read as weakening the claim since large hospitals still exceed small ones.

distractorRationale.A: Correct: cites all three data points and explicitly describes the rise-then-fall pattern that constitutes the non-linear relationship.
distractorRationale.B: Wrong (wrong comparison): compares only small to large hospitals, omitting the mid-size peak, which makes the relationship appear linear rather than non-linear.
distractorRationale.C: Wrong (incomplete evidence): shows only the upward portion of the trend (small to mid-size) without addressing the reversal at large hospitals, which is central to the non-linear claim.
distractorRationale.D: Wrong (misread value): presents the data points accurately but frames them in a way that emphasizes large hospitals still having higher rates than small ones, which could undermine rather than support the non-linear claim.

imageDescription: Bar chart titled 'Antibiotic Resistance Rates by Hospital Size.' X-axis: Hospital Size Category (Small: <100 beds, Mid-size: 100-300 beds, Large: >300 beds). Y-axis: Percentage of Infections Resistant to First-Line Antibiotics (0-40%). Bars: Small = 18%, Mid-size = 34%, Large = 24%. Source: National Epidemiology Surveillance Network, 2024.

chartData.title: Antibiotic Resistance Rates by Hospital Size

passageMetadata.source: Adapted from National Epidemiology Surveillance Network annual report
